content.text: Shared infrastructure costs that are invisible to the teams generating them produce a commons problem: every team has an incentive to instrument aggressively (because the cost falls on the platform budget, not their own) and no incentive to optimise. Telemetry chargeback — attributing the cost of log ingest, trace storage, and metric series to the team that generated them — changes this incentive structure without requiring the platform team to police every configuration.
The mechanism does not need to involve actual budget transfers (though it can). Even a monthly

showing each team's telemetry spend versus a soft budget target is sufficient to motivate optimisation. Teams that see they are consuming 3× their allocation will voluntarily reduce sampling rates and drop debug logs; teams that see they are well within budget will feel confident adding instrumentation when they need it.

attrs.alt: Telemetry chargeback model: from raw metrics to team cost reports

content.text: Chargeback requires that every log line, span, and metric series carries a

label that accurately identifies the generating team. This is enforced at onboarding (the platform rejects traffic without the label) and verified monthly. Given correctly-labelled data, usage measurement uses standard Prometheus queries against the collector's internal metrics.

# Total bytes ingested by Loki per team over the past 30 days
# loki_distributor_bytes_received_total is exported by the Loki distributor
sum by (team) (
  increase(loki_distributor_bytes_received_total[30d])
) / 1e9   # convert to GB

# Lines ingested (useful for per-line cost model)
sum by (team) (
  increase(loki_distributor_lines_received_total[30d])
) / 1e6   # convert to millions

# Total spans received by Tempo per team over 30 days
sum by (team) (
  increase(tempo_distributor_spans_received_total[30d])
) / 1e6   # convert to millions

# Bytes: useful if you're on a per-GB Tempo pricing model
sum by (team) (
  increase(tempo_distributor_bytes_received_total[30d])
) / 1e9

# Current active series per team (snapshot, not rate)
# Use the job label as a proxy for team if team label isn't on all metrics
count by (team) (
  {__name__=~".+"}
)

# Or via the Prometheus HTTP API for more accurate counts:
# GET /api/v1/query?query=count({team="genai-search"}) by (__name__)
Each signal type has a different unit cost. The numbers below are approximate for a self-hosted Grafana OSS stack on cloud VMs; adjust for your actual infrastructure pricing.

Platform overhead — the cost of Grafana, Alertmanager, the base collector fleet, and the platform team's own infrastructure — cannot be directly attributed to any single team. Three allocation models are commonly used:
Unfair: new small teams pay same as large ones
Fair: heavy users pay more overhead
Fixed monthly fee per onboarded team
Easy to budget; rewards volume growth

The proportional-to-variable-cost model (overhead × team's share of total log+trace+metric spend) is most commonly used because it naturally scales with the value each team gets from the platform: teams that generate more telemetry use more of the platform's fixed resources (dashboards, alerts, Grafana sessions).

The monthly chargeback report should run automatically on the first business day of each month, covering the prior calendar month. It is distributed via email to team engineering leads and posted to a shared Slack channel. It should include the raw numbers (log GB, span count, series count), the cost breakdown by signal type, the overhead share, the total versus budget, and a trend line showing the last three months.
When a team exceeds their telemetry budget, the response is a
. Cutting telemetry for a team that is investigating an incident is far more damaging than the cost overrun. The budget review process works as follows:
Platform team sends the over-budget notification with specific breakdown (is it logs, traces, or metrics that drove the overrun?)
Team has 5 business days to submit an optimisation plan or a budget increase request.

If overrun is > 25%: platform team schedules a 30-minute cost review meeting. Most overruns are caused by a single change (a new debug log statement, a cardinality regression, a feature that wasn't sampled).

If overrun persists 3 months with no plan: platform team applies a sampling floor (does not cut off the team, but limits healthy trace sample rate to a maximum consistent with the budget) until a fix is in place.

# Prometheus alert for teams approaching or exceeding their telemetry budget
# Requires a 'telemetry_budget_usd' metric loaded from budgets.yaml
- alert: TelemetryBudgetExceeded
  expr: |
    telemetry_cost_usd_monthly > on(team) group_left() telemetry_budget_usd
  for: 1d   # sustained over a day, not a transient spike
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "{{ $labels.team }} telemetry spend exceeds monthly budget"
    description: >
      Current spend: ${{ $value | printf "%.0f" }}.
      Budget: see telemetry_budget_usd{team="{{ $labels.team }}"}.
      Review: https://grafana.example.com/d/telemetry-costs

# Recording rule: compute monthly cost from raw metrics
- record: telemetry_cost_usd_monthly
  expr: |
    (
      sum by (team) (increase(loki_distributor_bytes_received_total[30d])) / 1e9
      * 0.031   # loki ingest + storage blended rate per GB
    )
    +
    (
      sum by (team) (increase(tempo_distributor_spans_received_total[30d])) / 1e6
      * 0.04    # tempo per million spans
    )
    +
    (
      count by (team) ({__name__=~".+"}) / 1e3
      * 0.015   # prometheus per 1K active series
    )
Most engineering organisations benefit from starting with

: cost visibility reports with soft budget targets, reviews for overruns, but no actual budget transfers between cost centres. This creates the right incentive (teams see their costs) without the administrative overhead of inter-team billing.

Hard chargeback — actual money moving between team budgets — is warranted when: (a) the organisation has multiple business units with separate P&Ls, (b) the absolute telemetry cost is significant enough to warrant accounting precision (typically > $50K/month platform-wide), or (c) repeated soft chargeback conversations have failed to change team behaviour.
Telemetry cost chargeback works by measuring per-team usage (Loki bytes ingested, Tempo spans received, Prometheus active series) using the

label that the platform enforces at onboarding, translating raw usage to cost using known unit rates, allocating platform overhead proportionally to variable cost, and distributing monthly reports to team leads. The report shows each signal's cost versus the team's budget allocation. Overruns trigger a structured review conversation — not an automatic cutoff — because cutting telemetry during an incident is more expensive than any cost overrun. Most organisations benefit from soft chargeback (visibility without actual budget transfers) rather than hard chargeback; the mere visibility of per-team cost is sufficient to motivate optimisation in the majority of cases.
